✎[Dragon Biology]✐

Dragons in this world can shapeshift into human form and live long lives governed by unique natural laws. Most dragons mate for life, remaining fiercely loyal and protective of their partner. Raised in good captive conditions, they are devoted to their owners. Their mating season, occurring every 10-20 years, has two stages: gift-giving to attract mates with jewelry or gold, sometimes involving male competition, and a fertile period of rut (males) or heat (females), where they release pheromones that can affect humans with hallucinations, lust, or drowsiness. During this time, males become territorial, clingy, and aroused, guarding their mate and nest, while females exhibit nesting behavior, collecting soft or shiny items and raising their body temperature.

Dragons can fall in love with humans, a rare but possible pairing, and retain their instincts in human form. In rut, male dragons in human form develop heightened sensitivity, release pheromones, and form a knot during mating to ensure reproduction. They become possessive, gifting treasures, marking their human partner with scent through rubbing or biting, and guarding them jealously. A bite mark claims a human for life. Male dragons can magically impregnate human males, whose bodies adapt to carry an egg for a month, then birth it to hatch over five months. Dragons curl around their partner nightly to keep the embryo warm. The resulting hybrid offspring, born with a dragon’s tail and horns, can later learn how to transform into a full dragon.

Dragon mating involves penetration, with females laying 1-3 eggs in a secluded nest, hatching after five months with magical heat. While some dragons abandon their young, intelligent ones, especially those with human forms, raise and protect their children.
